proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions are specific and consistent: the game explicitly calls itself a 'First-Person-Puzzle-Metroidvania' with an interconnected world that opens up through newly found upgrades and abilities, plus heavy emphasis on exploration, secret areas, environmental puzzle solving, and progression-gated traversal. The text repeatedly frames combat as a relatively small, sometimes puzzle-heavy component, which supports metroidvania over an action-led archetype. Detective cases and mystery-solving are notable topical layers, but the core gameplay identity is still ability-gated exploration in a handcrafted interconnected world.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Near-identical gameplay identity: first-person puzzle-metroidvania, versatile ability unlocks, dense secrets, toy-world traversal, minimal hand-holding, and light combat.
Same series formula and almost the same audience expectation: gated first-person exploration, puzzle-heavy progression, constant optional secrets, and even less combat emphasis.
A strong first-person metroidvania benchmark with interconnected progression and ability-gated backtracking, though it is more combat-driven and less puzzle-centric than Supraworld.
Shares first-person exploration, traversal-tool unlocks, revisiting areas, and secret hunting, but its structure leans more action-adventure than puzzle-metroidvania and supports co-op.
Standalone Supraland follow-up with the same first-person puzzle-metroidvania structure, traversal abilities, secret density, and playful environmental problem-solving.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Marathon as a 'PvPvE survival extraction FPS' and a 'PvP extraction shooter.' The core loop is clearly stated: infiltrate/scavenge zones, fight rival players and hostile AI, loot valuables, exfil, then use stolen loot and season-long unlocks to improve future runs. Supporting details include solo or crew-based runs, in-progress lobby insertion, finite supplies, ranked play, moddable weapons, build crafting, vault space expansion, and persistent seasonal progression. This strongly matches extraction_shooter over generic FPS labels or arena/hero shooter near-misses.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest genre benchmark: first-person extraction raids, PvPvE threats, gear loss on death, prep/loadout strategy, and progression driven by successful exfils. More hardcore and sim-leaning than Marathon, but still an essential comp.

Very close on match structure and tension: squad-or-solo PvPvE runs, contested objectives, hostile AI, rival teams, and extraction under pressure. Slower weapons and horror-western flavor differ, but the core loop fits strongly.
Sci-fi PvPvE extraction shooter focused on scavenging, contracts, rival players, and extract-with-loot progression.
Persistent extraction FPS with tactical looting, hostile factions, squad play, and survival-oriented exfil runs.
Loot-and-extract structure with other-player threat, persistent stash progression, and high-stakes session-based runs.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and concretely describe ARC Raiders as a third-person multiplayer extraction game: players enter distinct topside maps, scavenge loot, survive hostile machines and rival Raiders, then return to the underground hub/workshop to sell loot, craft, repair, upgrade stations, learn blueprints, level skills, and take trader quests. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvPvE pressure, repeated incursions with changing map conditions, and progression through gear, crafting, quests, and a branching skill tree. That combination strongly supports extraction_shooter as the core gameplay identity, with loot_shooter and tactical_shooter as secondary affinities rather than primary genre fits.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on core loop: sci-fi PvPvE drops for loot, hostile creatures plus players, extraction, contracts, and between-run gear progression. First-person perspective keeps it just short of a same-tier match.

Strong third-person extraction fit with scavenging runs, bring-home loot, and shelter upgrading. Slightly looser because it is more PvP-centric and less defined by co-op PvPvE enemy ecology than ARC Raiders.

A foundational extraction-shooter comp with raids, stash, traders, quests, and high-stakes loot loss. It is a bit less aligned due to first-person hardcore mil-sim emphasis and a more punishing sim/RPG layer than ARC Raiders.

Clear PvPvE extraction structure with AI threats, stealth/noise management, and squad extraction tension. Less close because its boss-hunt objective structure, old-weapons gunplay, and harsher permadeath flavor differ meaningfully from ARC Raiders' broader loot-progression loop.
Third-person sci-fi extraction shooter with machine threats, scavenging runs, PvPvE pressure, and persistent gear/base progression between sorties.

title: Call of Duty: Black Ops 7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a classic Call of Duty package centered on first-person gunplay: a co-op/solo campaign with covert military missions, competitive multiplayer with 6v6 and 20v20 maps, and zombies as an additional mode. The copy emphasizes near-future weaponry, mastering an arsenal, Omnimovement, multiplayer map counts, and a signature campaign/multiplayer structure, which strongly supports military_fps over horror-led archetypes. Zombies and psychological-war themes are present, but they read as one component of a broader shooter package rather than the core genre identity of the whole title.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Immediate Black Ops predecessor with the same campaign/multiplayer/Zombies bundle and closely related movement and gunplay fundamentals.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Call of Duty: Black Ops Cold War
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Call of Duty: Black Ops Cold War
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another Black Ops entry centered on a cinematic campaign, competitive multiplayer, and Treyarch Zombies as a unified package.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Shares the near-future Black Ops identity, mobility-forward combat, co-op campaign elements, and major Zombies emphasis.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare III
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare III
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest non-Black Ops Call of Duty comp as a modern full-package release with cinematic campaign, fast multiplayer progression, and cooperative side mode support.

unresolved_similar_games: Call of Duty: Black Ops II
missing_must_include_titles: Call of Duty: Black Ops Cold War

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored Steam description explicitly says 'Read a Romantic Comedy' and 'Standard Visual Novel Mechanics,' describing text reading, skipping previously read text, and hiding the text window to view artwork. The content is centered on an authored romance story with four heroines, no combat, and single-player progression through narrative scenes, which strongly matches the visual_novel archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Directly shares the same romance-VN structure, heroine-route focus, comedic writing style, and everyday dating progression.
Another contemporary romantic comedy visual novel built around choosing a heroine and following separate relationship routes.
Very similar lighthearted multi-heroine romance VN with slice-of-life scenes, route branching, and character-driven dating progression.
Shares the multi-route romantic comedy VN format, heavy character focus, and choice-driven progression through heroine stories.

title: Powerwash Simulator 2: Adventure Time Pack DLC

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is insufficient for taxonomy classification. The title suggests DLC for PowerWash Simulator 2, but no usable gameplay description, mechanics summary, perspective, session structure, or parent-game evidence is provided in the stored DB. The only stored genre/theme signal is a broad 'simulation' label from OpenCritic, which is too generic to map confidently to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The parent game and clearest match: the same pressure-washing jobs, first-person cleaning mechanics, and progression framework.
The original series foundation with the same satisfying wash-nozzle loop, grime-clearing objectives, and methodical job structure.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: PowerWash Simulator - Spongebob Squarepants Special Pack
staged_neighbors.requested_title: PowerWash Simulator - SpongeBob SquarePants Special Pack
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another themed crossover DLC built around the exact same cleaning gameplay, just with licensed environments and fan-service framing.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: PowerWash Simulator - Back to the Future Special Pack
staged_neighbors.requested_title: PowerWash Simulator - Back to the Future Special Pack
staged_neighbors.why_similar: A recognizable licensed add-on that keeps the core wash-and-complete mission flow intact while swapping in franchise-specific locations.
Another franchise crossover for the same simulator formula, emphasizing familiar cleaning mechanics on bespoke themed maps.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored evidence clearly describes a standalone single-player puzzle game built around dual-layer nonogram and jigsaw challenges across 250+ puzzles, with calming presentation and difficulty ramping. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a general nonogram/jigsaw puzzle category, and the game does not materially fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, or any other listed archetype. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on the core nonogram logic loop, large puzzle volume, accessibility options, and steady progression; it lacks the anchor’s jigsaw second phase.
Another substantial nonogram-focused puzzler with cozy presentation, lots of content, and approachable brain-training cadence, though without the jigsaw assembly half.
Strong match for the calm, art-restoration, puzzle-by-puzzle jigsaw side of the anchor, but it misses the nonogram deduction that defines half the gameplay identity.
Relaxed artistic jigsaw puzzling with a stronger challenge curve than the original, making it a meaningful neighbor on the visual assembly side only.
Focused nonogram logic progression with lots of handcrafted puzzles and a calm solo brain-training feel.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Minos as a maze-building roguelite centered on designing and redesigning labyrinth layouts, placing walls/gates/corridors, linking traps through pressure-plate chain reactions, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. The clearest gameplay identity is systemic construction and trap engineering rather than direct action combat, which aligns best with engineering_sandbox_sim. Roguelite run structure, procedural variation ('Every run brings new enemies, new traps'), and single-player premium replayability are all directly supported. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not stated, so those fields are left blank.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Centered on building lethal maze-like outposts packed with traps and routing intruders through killboxes.
A roguelite twist on reverse dungeon defense where you prepare traps and monsters to stop invading heroes.
Focuses on constructing and defending an evil dungeon, placing rooms, traps, and defenses against intruders.
Emphasizes chaining traps and environmental hazards to wipe out incoming waves, though with less maze-building emphasis.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator copy consistently describe a third-person action-RPG structure: exploring the area around Zoz, helping townspeople through quests, real-time slash-and-dodge combat with a machete, companions with unique combat skills, cooking for buffs, bond-building with residents, and magical weapon/tool enhancement. The strongest fit is a fantasy action RPG with quest exploration and social/progression layers. 'Open world' is not stated outright, so confidence is moderate rather than maximal, but the overall gameplay identity aligns best with open_world_fantasy_action_rpg over alternatives like loot_action_rpg or monster_hunter_style_action_rpg, which lack strong loot-chase or boss-hunt evidence here.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on action RPG combat, cooking/farming support systems, town questing, allies, and helping residents in a cozy fantasy settlement, though Harvestella is broader and more life-sim/farming driven.

Strong overlap in fighting monsters for ingredients, returning home to restore a family restaurant, and food-centered progression, though its dungeon-crawling roguelite structure is more dominant than the anchor's social questing.

Good fit for light action combat, party support, town relationships, cooking, and community tasks, but farming/life-sim systems and broader daily-routine structure are more central than in the anchor.
Town-focused action RPG loop built around gathering ingredients, treating residents, and improving relationships through helping the community.
Blends real-time combat, town questing, farming/crafting, and deep social bonds with villagers in a tighter life-sim RPG structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a short, low-stress single-player fishing-focused experience with handcrafted art, river drifting, landmark observation, and decisions that influence the outcome. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a fitting fishing-sim or contemplative narrative-sim bucket, and the evidence does not strongly support mapping it to an existing archetype such as life_sim or visual_novel without overreaching. The title appears to center on relaxed fishing and light branching narrative rather than the defining gameplay identities represented in the taxonomy.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Fishing remains the core loop and it shares a relaxing, narrative-driven structure with character interactions, though it is more explicitly RPG/progression-led than the anchor.
Very close in low-stress, short, cozy fishing feel and atmosphere, but its garden-building sandbox layer makes it less purely contemplative and story-outcome driven.
A clear cozy fishing-led narrative game, but its town restoration, cooking, shop management, upgrades, and co-op broaden it into a fuller slice-of-life RPG loop than the anchor.

Fishing and discovery are central and it also pairs them with unsettling narrative undercurrents, but the upgrade economy, navigation risk, and survival pressure make it much tenser and more systems-heavy.
Shares the low-stress, small-scale, contemplative feel and light fishing activity, with exploration and character moments over challenge.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Prop Sumo as a 'party fighting game' centered on PvP matches where players brawl with melee moves, chain light and heavy attacks, use items, and compete for score across arena maps. The Steam detailed description adds strong fighting-game signals: 1v1 and free-for-all match formats, a Training Dojo to master moves and combos, unlockable ultimates, and round-based progression. This supports a primary identity of traditional_fighter, with party_game as a secondary flavor due to chaotic multiplayer arenas, items, and friend-focused free-for-all play.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on party-first PvP brawling: round-based chaos, items, interactive stages, local/online play, and casual-competitive free-for-all energy.
Excellent match for hazardous arena melee and knockout-driven party sessions, though its floppy physics focus is looser and less combo-structured than Prop Sumo.
Major benchmark for multiplayer arena fighting with items, stage hazards, free-for-alls, and custom rules; less close in perspective, movement model, and fighter precision.
Short-round party combat with pickups, hazards, simple inputs, and score-based multiplayer maps fits well, but its ranged boomerang-centric combat is a step away from Prop Sumo's melee brawling.
Structured versus combat with training, combos, item spawns, rules variants, and strong 1v1/free-for-all support.

current_live_neighbors.title: Blade Arcus From Shining: Battle Arena

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Beyond Words as a word-based strategic puzzle roguelite: 'fusion of roguelike strategy and word-crafting,' with runs shaped by letter placement, modifiers, stickers, booster/power cards, upgrades, synergies, seeded runs, time attacks, and boss challenges. The gameplay loop is tactical board optimization and build construction across repeated runs, with no combat signals. Strong fit for word_puzzle_strategy rather than action, simulation, or hidden-object categories.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very solid fit on word-building as the primary verb plus upgrades, endless mode, and optimization, though it is framed more as turn-based word combat/RPG than a tactical roguelite board-planning puzzle.
Shares word construction, upgrades, items, bosses, and compact repeatable challenge structure, but its action-RPG combat focus is a noticeable step away from Beyond Words’ puzzle-first run planning.
Excellent match for run-based synergy stacking, rule manipulation, seeded/challenge play, and score optimization, but it lacks the anchor’s wordplay and board-letter puzzle identity.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Incantation as a first-person horror mystery game centered on exploring an eerie cult village, searching for clues, hiding from danger, and uncovering the truth behind a missing daughter. The repeated emphasis on fear, madness, mystery-solving, and stealth-based survival supports psychological_horror as the best fit, with stealth_action_adventure as a secondary influence. Evidence for combat-driven survival-horror is weak, so the classification stays with horror exploration and stealth rather than action combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match in first-person vulnerable horror: exploration, puzzle solving, stealthy evasion, sanity/fear pressure, and a maternal narrative drive all align well.

Strong fit on first-person psychological horror, missing-person investigation, environmental clue-finding, and story-led exploration, though its dog-assisted forest investigation is a bit more guided and less stealth-centric.

Good overlap in first-person horror, cult threat, stealth avoidance, and mystery unraveling, but its sound-based enemy system and survival-horror framing make it somewhat more mechanically specialized.
Story-heavy first-person psychological horror with environmental clues, family tragedy, ritual themes, and East Asian occult framing.
First-person Thai horror focused on stealth, evasion, and piecing together a supernatural mystery rather than fighting back.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes a single-player, third-person, narrative-driven 3D adventure centered on traversal and physics-inspired puzzle solving in thematic worlds, with dialogue and an emotional grief/legacy storyline. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a clear home for a non-combat narrative puzzle-adventure of this type. The evidence is strong for gameplay identity, but it does not strongly support any allowed primary archetype without forcing a misleading fit such as action-adventure or hidden-object puzzle.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: third-person single-player exploration, environmental puzzles, traversal, and a symbolic emotional journey with minimal combat emphasis.
Strong match on grief-focused narrative adventure and world-manipulation puzzle progression, though its time-control platforming structure is a bit more directed than the anchor.
Good alignment on non-combat puzzle-adventure, healing themes, and progression through stylized realms, though it is somewhat lighter and more compact in structure.
Shares dreamlike stages, emotional storytelling, and world-rule manipulation puzzles, but it is more abstract and self-contained, with less exploratory third-person adventure feel.
Story-led 3D platform-puzzle adventure with light traversal, environmental obstacles, and a melancholic emotional journey rather than combat.

removed_candidates: Brothers: A Tale of Two Sons

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a multiplayer-focused Pokémon battle product centered on traditional Pokémon battle mechanics, with Ranked, Casual, and Private Battles plus Pokémon HOME compatibility. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a strong fit for a match-based turn-based monster battling game without the broader campaign, exploration, or creature-collection loop expected by monster_collect_rpg. Accepting it under an existing archetype would overstate unsupported elements such as adventure structure or collection gameplay.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Battle-focused Pokémon spinoff centered on traditional turn-based matches, multiplayer, cups, and imported teams; closest structural match to a standalone Pokémon battle platform.
Shares the modern Pokémon battle system, competitive formats, and team-building logic, but the core game is still a full adventure RPG with catching and exploration.
Uses current-generation Pokémon battle mechanics and online play, but its open-world exploration and collection-heavy campaign push it farther from Champions’ match-based focus.
Shares the same core Pokémon battle mechanics and competitive team-building logic, but its primary loop is still campaign exploration and progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently define The Occultist as a first-person narrative thriller with explicit survival-horror framing: 'Survival horror: The Occultist has all the distinctive elements of the genre,' combined with tense exploration, supernatural threats, ominous cult imagery, and a horror-focused abandoned-island setting. The gameplay loop centers on exploring Godstone, investigating the protagonist's father's disappearance and occult rituals, using a mystic pendulum to interact with and modify the environment, and crucially avoiding direct combat: 'Alan always avoids direct combat' and progresses via 'Hide and Sneak' stealth mechanics. That strongly supports survival_horror as the primary identity, with psychological_horror and stealth_action_adventure as meaningful secondary overlaps.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: first-person survival horror built around vulnerability, exploration, chase tension, and fleeing rather than fighting.
Strong match on first-person survival horror, stealth avoidance, cult/supernatural menace, and narrative progression with minimal direct combat.
Good overlap in first-person horror, environmental exploration, and oppressive supernatural tension, but it is slower and more psychological than stealth-survival focused.
First-person survival horror built around hiding, sneaking, and surviving a lethal pursuer with minimal combat agency.
First-person survival horror with resource pressure, environmental problem-solving, and constant avoidance of a stalking threat.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Samson as a single-player open-world urban action game, not a pure racer. The core loop centers on moving through open world city districts, taking consequential jobs/missions under a debt-and-time-pressure structure, engaging in close-quarters brawler combat, and using cars as traversal/combat tools during chases and collisions. Repeated signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' climbing through alleys and rooftops, a 25+ upgrade skill tree, law-response escalation, and story-driven progress tied to Samson's sister and criminal debt. Racing is present, but the evidence frames driving as one major verb inside a broader open-world action-adventure campaign rather than the sole governing loop.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent fit: open-world city structure, story-driven crime missions, strong hand-to-hand combat emphasis, driving integrated into progression, and police heat all align closely with Samson’s stated loop.

Very strong neighbor for the mix of brutal melee, upgrade progression, open-world roaming, and vehicles used directly as combat tools, though its desert survival framing is less urban and less mission-consequence driven.
Story-driven open-world crime campaign with mission pressure, police escalation, urban driving, and brutal close-range combat.
Grounded city crime sandbox mixing driving, on-foot fights, wanted-level pressure, and a focused personal story.
Open-city mission structure with constant driving, street-level combat, faction conflict, and player-driven urban chaos.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as western_narrative_rpg using DLC inheritance guidance. Terran Armada is explicitly described as a story DLC for Starfield, adding new locations, technologies, weapons, and enemy encounters, with no indication of a mode or genre shift. The parent game's stored descriptions strongly establish the core identity: Bethesda-style single-player sci-fi RPG with character creation, faction and side quests, dialogue-driven roleplay, open-ended planetary exploration, ship piloting/combat, loot/crafting, and outpost building across a long-form campaign. That evidence supports a quest-driven open-world western narrative RPG more strongly than a pure shooter or action-adventure classification. The DLC copy is additive and fully consistent with that parent gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct parent game of this story DLC; same systems, structure, progression, and audience expectation.
Very close Bethesda open-world RPG formula: first-person exploration, quest hubs, loot, shooting, crafting, and player-driven builds.
Single-player first-person sci-fi RPG with dialogue choices, companions, ranged combat, and quest-driven progression; somewhat smaller-scale than Starfield.
Spacefaring action RPG with exploration, missions, combat, and progression, but it is more directed and squad/story focused than Bethesda's sandbox RPG loop.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim - Special Edition
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim Special Edition
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest non-sci-fi match to Bethesda's open-world first-person quest RPG structure, with freeform exploration, faction questlines, and build-driven progression.

removed_candidates: The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim VR

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies ChainStaff as a 'brutal action-platformer' and '2D Platformer' with a transforming spear/grappling weapon, swinging and traversal, mutating hordes, and 'bone-shaking boss fights.' The core loop is side-scrolling combat plus platforming through a handcrafted campaign. The text also supports a single-player premium campaign structure, hand-drawn surreal presentation, grotesque sci-fi/horror setting, and some authored branching via rescue-or-harvest choices leading to different endings. Evidence is strong for action_platformer and does not strongly support a different primary archetype such as metroidvania or character_action.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: brutal 2D action-platforming, grotesque sci-fi enemies, heavy boss focus, weapon upgrades, and a combat-first campaign structure line up strongly with ChainStaff.
Another gory 2D action-platformer with fast melee combat and a nasty hand-drawn style, but it leans more into combo-driven character-action than ChainStaff’s traversal-tool-centered combat loop.
Shares brutal side-scrolling combat, grotesque enemies, upgrades, and memorable bosses, but its non-linear metroidvania structure and exploration emphasis make it a looser fit.
Linear 2D action-platformer centered on a chain-hook arm for swinging, grappling, combat mobility, and set-piece boss encounters.
Fast, stage-based action-platforming with hook-assisted traversal, melee-focused combat, and tough boss-driven pacing.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify a single-player action RPG with randomized worlds, roguelike run structure, fast dodge-heavy combat, bosses, weapons/abilities/artifacts, per-run upgrades, and permanent meta progression. That strongly supports an action-RPG identity with replayable build variation. The main ambiguity is taxonomy fit: the game reads like a single-player action roguelite, but that exact archetype is not available in the allowed list, so loot_action_rpg is the closest supported primary archetype, with character_action as secondary due to fast aggressive dodge-based combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Run-based action roguelite with fast dodging, aggressive real-time combat, build-shaping upgrades, and persistent progression between attempts.
Element-focused roguelite combat with quick dashes, randomized runs, relic loadouts, and heavy emphasis on ability combinations.
Top-down hack-and-slash roguelite with brisk movement, dodge-centric fights, run upgrades, and artifact-driven build variation.
Action roguelike centered on repeat runs, hero ability builds, environmental exploration, and meta-style progression in a fantasy setting.
Action RPG/roguelite hybrid with persistent progression, dungeon runs, loot/upgrades, and a stronger adventure structure than pure arcade roguelikes.
